name: Grass-Cutting Sword
description: The Grass-Cutting Sword, also known as Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi (草薙の剣) or Ame-no-Murakumo-no-Tsurugi (天叢雲剣), is one of the Three Sacred Treasures of Japan, representing valor. According to legend, the sword was discovered by the storm god Susanoo when he slew the eight-headed serpent Yamata no Orochi. Inside the serpent's tail, he found a magnificent sword. Susanoo presented the sword to the goddess Amaterasu as a reconciliation gift after a long period of conflict. The sword's name, 'Grass-Cutting Sword,' derives from an incident where Yamato Takeru, a legendary prince, used the sword to cut down grass that had been set ablaze by his enemies, revealing a path to escape. The sword is said to possess the power to control the wind and to grant invincibility to its wielder. It is believed to be housed at the Atsuta Shrine in Nagoya, although it has never been publicly displayed, adding to its mystique and legendary status. Its importance lies not only in its mythical origins but also in its role as a symbol of imperial authority and national identity within Japanese culture. The sword represents the emperor's connection to the divine and the legitimacy of his rule. Its presence, even unseen, reinforces the emperor's position as the head of state and a descendant of the gods. The Grass-Cutting Sword continues to be a potent symbol in modern Japan, appearing in various forms of popular culture, from anime and manga to video games and literature, ensuring its enduring legacy.
subtitle: One of Japan's Three Sacred Treasures
origin: Found inside the tail of the slain serpent Yamata no Orochi

keyMyths.title: Susanoo and the Yamata no Orochi
keyMyths.description: Susanoo slays the eight-headed serpent, Yamata no Orochi, and discovers the sword in one of its tails. He then presents the sword to his sister, Amaterasu, as a peace offering.

keyMyths.title: Yamato Takeru's Escape
keyMyths.description: Prince Yamato Takeru uses the sword to cut down the burning grass, revealing a path to safety and giving the sword its name. This demonstrates the sword's power and its ability to aid its wielder in times of peril.

sources.author: Basil Hall Chamberlain
sources.description: Translation of the Kojiki, an early Japanese chronicle of myths and legends.
sources.text: Translation of the Kojiki, an early Japanese chronicle of myths and legends.

sources.author: W.G. Aston
sources.work: Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697
sources.description: Translation of the Nihon Shoki, another early Japanese chronicle.
sources.text: Translation of the Nihon Shoki, another early Japanese chronicle.
